nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and standing tropical freshwaters — backwaters, swamps, ponds, and small streams in the lowland West African forest belt.
nativeRange.biotope: West African lowland blackwater / soft tropical freshwater

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs near the bulb
open midground / background footprint with vertical headroom

avoidWith: fish that uproot bulbs (e.g. large barbs in groups)

summary: A West African bulb plant grown for its dramatic red-bronze spotted leaves; produces both submerged and floating lily-pad foliage, and is generally considered an easy beginner-friendly centrepiece.
careNotes: Nymphaea zenkeri is the West African 'Red Tiger Lotus' commonly sold in the aquarium trade as 'Tiger Lotus' or 'Red Tiger Lotus'. It is closely related to — and frequently confused with — the larger Nymphaea lotus (the broader African / Egyptian form), and Tropica historically markets the red zenkeri form under the N. lotus 'Red' label. For AquaMate's purposes this entry covers the typical aquarium red-leaved zenkeri bulb. The plant is a bulb-bearing species: bury only about the bottom half to one-third of the bulb in the substrate, leaving the growing tip exposed; fully burying the bulb tends to cause it to rot. Like other Nymphaea, it produces two leaf forms — heart-shaped submerged leaves near the bulb and long-stemmed floating lily pads that reach the surface and can canopy the tank. To keep the plant in a compact, fully-submerged form, trim long stems at the base before they reach the surface; otherwise allow a few stems through and the plant will create surface shade. Strong red coloration is generally tied to higher light and ample iron — under low light the leaves tend to be more bronze or green. Generally advised to keep around 22–28 °C in soft to moderately hard water (pH ≈ 6.0–7.5). CO2 is not necessary but has been reported to increase leaf size and intensify colour. Source spread on temperature was modest (Modern Aquarium 21.7–30 °C, hobbyist consensus ~22–28 °C); height varies widely depending on whether the keeper allows floating pads (Tropica notes 20–30+ cm submerged; full plants with surface pads in tall tanks can substantially exceed that). Roots and bulbs anchor strongly in nutrient-rich substrate and respond well to root tabs.
breedingNotes: Propagates readily in the aquarium via daughter bulbs that form on the parent bulb and via side shoots that can be separated and replanted. Mature plants whose floating leaves reach an open-top aquarium can flower (white-to-yellow blooms) and set seed, which can also be collected and germinated, but vegetative propagation from offsets is the typical hobby method.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied Tropica search URL was resolved to the species page at plantdetails/4432/4432, which Tropica lists under 'Nymphaea lotus' but is widely understood in the trade as the red zenkeri form. Tropica rates light demand as medium, CO2 demand as low, growth rate as medium, difficulty as medium, height 20–30+ cm, origin West Africa.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked planting technique (push ~1/3 of bulb into substrate), dual submerged/floating leaf form, midground-to-background placement, medium-to-high light for red coloration, daughter-bulb propagation, fast growth and Easy Green / Easy Root Tab / iron fertilization guidance.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Nymphaeaceae), African origin, pH 6.0–7.5, moderate light requirement, medium growth rate, propagation via side shoots, and the bronze-vs-red colour shift under low vs high light.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature range (71–86 °F ≈ 21.7–30 °C), Nymphaeaceae family, moderate-to-strong light, midground-to-background placement, fast growth, heavy-root-feeder behaviour. Notes that the common name 'Tiger Lotus' is applied to both N. lotus and N. zenkeri in the trade.
